coredata.dc:title: Assessment of thermal conductivity of polyethylene glycol-carbon dot nanofluid through a combined experimental-data mining investigation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Shahsavar, Amin
Shaham, Aidin
Mirzaei, Mohamad Amin
Jamei, Mehdi
Seifikar, Fatemeh
Azizian, Saeid
coredata.dc:description:
The aim of this study is to determine the effect of nanoparticle concentration
( φ )
and temperature on the thermal conductivity of polyethylene glycol (PEG)-carbon dot nanofluid (NF). The considered range for temperature and
φ
is 20–60 °C and 0–7%, respectively. The results indicated an ascending trend of NF thermal conductivity with boosting both temperature and
φ
. The percentage of increase in thermal conductivity of NF with temperature and
φ
compared to the base fluid was in the range of 7.23–13.43% and 75.08–85.17%, respectively. Moreover, two efficient data-driven approaches, namely Multi-variate linear regression (MLR) and response surface methodology (RSM) schemes were developed to simulate the thermal conductivity of the PEG-carbon dot NF and fit the predictive relationships. The outcomes of modeling revealed that RSM model (R = 0.984 and RMSE = 0.013 W/m.K), due to taking into account the interaction between volume fraction and temperature, yielded more accuracy than MLR (R = 0.960 and RMSE = 0.021 W/m.K).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot nanofluid
Correlation
Multi-linear regression
Polyethylene glycol
Response surface methodology
Thermal conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematics of the carbon NFs preparation method.
(a) The TEM image of the synthesized carbon NFs and (b) its corresponding particle size distribution histogram.

(a) DLS histogram of the CDs NFs, 10 times diluted with PEG and (b) Schematic representation of the dispersed CD in PEG and its hydrodynamic diameter,
d H
.
The workflow of the RSM approach.

Changes of thermal conductivity of PEG-CQDs NF versus temperature in terms of
φ
.

Changes of thermal conductivity of PEG-CQDs NF versus
φ
in terms of temperature.

Changes of thermal conductivity ratio versus temperature in terms of
φ
.
Comparison between experimental and predicted thermal conductivity of NF in form of scatter plots (Left side) and variation data points (Right side).
Probability distribution function of the relative deviation for couple modeling of thermal conductivity.
Relative deviation (RD) 3D contours for thermal conductivity modeling using MLR and RSM approaches versus volume fraction and temperature parameters.
The outcome of MLR and RSM analysis for prediction of thermal conductivity of NF.
Evaluating the Goodness of fit of MLR and RSM models for prediction of thermal conductivity of NF.

head.ce:title: Assessment of thermal conductivity of polyethylene glycol-carbon dot nanofluid through a combined experimental-data mining investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Kermanshah University of Technology, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Kermanshah University of Technology, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The aim of this study is to determine the effect of nanoparticle concentration
( φ )
and temperature on the thermal conductivity of polyethylene glycol (PEG)-carbon dot nanofluid (NF). The considered range for temperature and
φ
is 20–60 °C and 0–7%, respectively. The results indicated an ascending trend of NF thermal conductivity with boosting both temperature and
φ
. The percentage of increase in thermal conductivity of NF with temperature and
φ
compared to the base fluid was in the range of 7.23–13.43% and 75.08–85.17%, respectively. Moreover, two efficient data-driven approaches, namely Multi-variate linear regression (MLR) and response surface methodology (RSM) schemes were developed to simulate the thermal conductivity of the PEG-carbon dot NF and fit the predictive relationships. The outcomes of modeling revealed that RSM model (R = 0.984 and RMSE = 0.013 W/m.K), due to taking into account the interaction between volume fraction and temperature, yielded more accuracy than MLR (R = 0.960 and RMSE = 0.021 W/m.K).

ce:section.ce:para: Nanofluids (NFs), which result from the distribution of nanoscale particles in common coolants, are a novel type of fluids with exceptional potential for improving the performance of heat transfer systems. These particles are mainly made of metal particles, metal oxides and carbon nanotubes.

Due to the drawbacks of using traditional fluids and even microfluids, including sedimentation, erosion, fouling, and boosted pressure loss of the flow, the researchers turned to study NFs. In 1995, the idea of NFs was mooted by Choi [1], and a major revolution in heat transfer in fluids emerged. In fact, a new look at the suspension of fluid with solid nanoparticles was proposed. Also, because of the small size of the nanoparticles, a lot of corrosion, impurities and pressure loss problems were reduced and the stability of fluids against sedimentation was considerably enhanced [2,3].

The higher thermal conductivity of nanoparticles used in NFs, compared to the thermal conductivity of base fluids, makes the thermal conductivity and, consequently, the cooling capability of NFs better than conventional coolants [4–6]. This issue has led to a significant acceptance of NFs by researchers leading to extensive laboratory and numerical research on the synthesis of new NFs [7–9], thermophysical properties of NFs [10–12] and cooling performance of NFs in various applications such as batteries [13–15], CPUs [16–18], heat exchangers [19–21], solar systems [22–24], cavities [25–27] and engines [28–30]. Also, many researchers have tried to combine NFs with other methods of improving heat transfer, such as fin [31–33], turbulator [34–36], magnetic field [37–39], and porous media [40–42].

As a new class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have shown a high capability to use as multi-purpose materials with variety of applications [43] and gained the attention of many researchers. Moreover, the possibility of functionalization of CDs’ surface makes researcher to control their physicochemical properties [43]. Added to optical and fluorescent properties, CDs exhibit environmentally friendly and have low-toxicity, low-price, and simple synthesis procedure; CDs are utilized in bio- and chemical sensors, nanomedicine, bioluminescence imaging, photo-catalyst, electrical catalysts [43], thermal conductivity enhancing [44], solar thermal conversion [45], and heat transfer [43]. CDs are synthesized by different methods, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical and chemical oxidation, ultrasonic and microwave irradiation, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal synthesis, and template-based method, [46]. Some of the challenges of NFs are lack of durability, variation of base fluid properties, and high cost. However, microwave-assisted synthesis of CDs in polyethylene glycol (PEG) as a facile and efficient method leads to the production of a green and stable CDs NF [45].

A review of previously published studies in the field of NFs shows no study has been done on the thermal conductivity of PEG-CD NFs. For this reason, the present study is performed to measure and report thermal conductivity of PEG-CD NF at different temperatures and
φ
. Also, two data-driven models, namely Multi-variate linear regression (MLR) and response surface methodology (RSM) are adapted to the simulation of thermal conductivity of the PEG-CD NF. Various statistical indices and graphical validation tools are employed to compare the results obtained by both approaches. In addition, two reliable relationships are correlated to the estimation of the thermal conductivity values in acceptable precision.

The colloidal carbonaceous nanomaterials were quickly produced by the one-step microwave irradiation method [47–50]. Briefly, liquid PEG 200 (70 ml) was placed in the microwave oven (Bitron, model: BMW-25BGC) and illuminated for 10 min at the power of 700 W (Fig. 1 .). As PEG consists of many dipoles, the microwave electric field interacts with these dipoles, causing heat generation in the sample. Therefore, during microwave heating, the temperature of the sample approaches about 300 °C, resulting in PEG carbonization. This is the time when the colorless sample changes to a dark one, revealing the carbon nanomaterials are formed. As the synthesized carbon nanoparticles are dispersed in non-reacted PEG, the obtained colloidal material is carbon NFs.

Characterization of the prepared carbon NFs was followed by Philips EM208s transmission electron microscope (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern Instruments Ltd, Zetasizer Ver. 7.11). The histogram of the particles size distribution for the dispersed carbon particles in the prepared NF was obtained based on the TEM image.

Fig. 2 a illustrates the TEM image of the synthesized dispersed carbon particles, indicating the formation of spherical nanoparticles with different diameters (d < 30 nm). Based on the obtained histogram (Fig. 2b), most of the carbon nanoparticles have diameter of less than 10 nm with an average of 7.5 nm. These results obviously reveal the formation of CDs by microwave illumination of PEG.

Based on the DLS analysis, the distribution of hydrodynamic size of CDs dispersed in the diluted NFs was obtained (Fig. 3 a). According to Fig. 3a, the average hydrodynamic diameter
(
d H
)
of CDs dispersed in PEG is ∼91 nm while the average diameter
( d )
of the CDs obtained by TEM is ∼7.5 nm. This is due to this fact that
d H
corresponds to the diameter of the CD core and its protective shell (Fig. 3b). The high stability of dispersed CDs in PEG can be explained by the high thickness of the protective shell (∼42 nm).

In this work, the well-known KD2 pro instrument (Decagon devices, Inc., USA) equipped with the KS-1 probe was employed to determine the thermal conductivity of the PEG-CD NF. This equipment, which operates according to the hot wire method, measures and provides the thermal conductivity of fluids in a short time of 90 s.

To bring the NF temperature to the desired value, the container containing the NF was placed in a constant temperature bath. The temperature of NF was measured using three K-type thermocouples that were placed at three different depths inside the container containing the NF. After making sure that the NF temperature has reached the desired temperature and remains at that temperature for 30 min, the constant temperature bath was turned off and the NF thermal conductivity was measured 5 times. The reason for turning off the constant temperature bath when measuring the thermal conductivity of NFs was that the KD2 pro instrument is extremely sensitive and there should be no movement in the NF as it changes the amount of thermal conductivity. It should be noted that the amount of thermal conductivity reported in the present study at each
φ
and temperature is the average of the 5 values obtained from the tests for that case. Before each test, the device is calibrated using the glycerin sample provided by Decagon company. The results showed that the uncertainty of the device is less than
±
3.4%.

One of the most popular linear-based regression data-driven methods is the multi-linear regression (MLR) approach, widely used for modeling engineering phenomena. This approach can generate the optimal relationship between the dependent and its output independent variables. This mechanism obtains the best linear polynomial relationship by minimizing the least-squares function between actual and predicted datasets. The typical formula of MLR is expressed as [51]:
(1)
π =
a 0
+
a 1
x 1
+
a 2
x 2
+ … +
a l
x l
+ ψ
where
π
is the objective parameter,
x k
is the kth independent parameters,
a k
denotes the kth coefficient owing to the least-squares, and
ψ
is related to the stochastic regression error. Here, for gaining the best-fitting relationship of thermal conductivity
( k )
, two critical variables, namely, volume concentration
( φ )
and temperature (T) were taken into account for modeling as [51]:
(2)
k =
a 0
+
a 1
φ +
a 2
T

RSM is a set of mathematical and statistical techniques for simultaneously assessing the relationships between a set of independent (experimental) variables (observations) and one or more targets. The mechanism of this technique is optimally providing a relationship between a set of input variables and a target. The hypothetical mathematical model obtained from this method includes a second-order polynomial expression obtained based on ANOVA. The chosen designed observations fit a relationship as [52]:
(3)
Y =
A 0
+
∑
i = 1
K
A i
X i
+
∑
i = 1
K
∑
j = 1 , j ≠ i
K
A
i j
X i
X j
+
∑
i = 1
K
A
i i
X i
2
+ α
where
Y
denotes predicted target,
α
is constant intercept term,
X i
is the ith independent variable,
A 0
is a constant,
A i
,
A
i i
and
A
i j
are linear terms, quadratic term, and interactive terms, respectively [53]. Fig. 4 shows the schematic view of the RSM model for the prediction task in the current research.

For assessing the ability of the provided data-driven models, four statistical indices were examined, including the correlation coefficient (R), Willmott's index of agreement (I
A ), root mean squared error (RMSE), and mean absolute percentage of relative error (MAPE). The formulation of considered statistical indices is defined as [54–56]:
(4)
R =
∑
i = 1
N
(
k
p , i
−
π ¯
p
)
.

k
o , i

k ¯
o
)

i = 1
N

k
p , i
−
k ¯
p
)
2
∑
i N
(
k
o , i

o
)
2
(5)
I A
= 1 −
∑
i N
(
π
o , i
−
k i
)

i = 1
N

|

o
)

0 <
I A
≤ 1
(6)
R M S E =
(
1 N

i = 1
N

)

)
0.5
(7)

(
100 N
)

i = 1
N

−
k p,i
k o,i
|
where N is the number of the dataset,
k o,i
and
k p,i
are the ith experimental and predicted thermal conductivity of hybrid NF, also,
k o
¯
and
k p
¯
are the average values of the experimental and predicted conductivity of hybrid NF.

This section consists of two sub-sections, the first of which is dedicated to providing NF thermal conductivity measured for different values of temperature (20–60 °C) and volume fractions of nanoparticles (0–7%) and the second dedicated to developing an accurate model for predicting NF thermal conductivity using the RSM method.

Fig. 5 gives the impact of temperature on the thermal conductivity of PEG-CQDs NF at different
φ
values. The increasing trend of thermal conductivity of NF with temperature is evident. An increase in temperature from 20 °C to 60 °C at
φ
= 0% and
φ
= 7% is accompanied by a 7.23% and 13.43% increase in thermal conductivity of NF. Increasing the temperature enhances the Brownian motion and, consequently, the kinetic energy of the nanoparticles, which increases the thermal conductivity by increasing the collision between the particles and enhancing the micro-convection in the liquid molecules around the nanoparticles.

Fig. 6 discloses the influence of
φ
on the thermal conductivity of PEG-CQDs NF at different temperatures. It is evident that the rise of
φ
entails an increment in the thermal conductivity of NF, which is because of the increase of the number of nanoparticles suspended in the base fluid. For instance, at temperatures of 20 °C and 60 °C, increasing the
φ
from 0% to 7% increases the thermal conductivity of NF by 75.08% and 85.17%, respectively.

The effect of
φ
and temperature on the ratio of thermal conductivity of NF to the thermal conductivity of base fluid is shown in Fig. 7 . As seen, the conductivity ratio is always greater than one and its value is greater in larger values of
φ
and temperature. At
φ
= 1%,
φ
= 3% and
φ
= 7%, the conductivity ratio varies over the range of 1.32–1.34, 1.48–1.53 and 1.75–1.85, respectively. The maximum conductivity ratio is 1.85, which belongs to the case of
φ
= 7% and
T
= 60 °C.

In this study, two data-driven models, namely MLP and RSM approaches, were developed to accurately estimate the thermal conductivity of hybrid NFs. Both models were formulated in Matlab 2020b, and the simulating procedure was performed using a PC system via a Core(TM) i7-6700 CPU @ 3.20 GHz processor and 16 GB RAM. Here, the outcomes of the MLR and RSM models were obtained based on the P-value, standard error (SE), and t-statistic (t-Stat) metric as listed in Table 1 . Besides, both data-driven models yield two optimal linear and quadratic relationships, respectively for estimation of thermal conductivity of hybrid NFs as expressed in Eqs. (8) and (9):
(8)
K | M L R : 0.2038 + 0.02072 φ + 0.0.000687 T
(9)
K | R S M : 0.192 + 0.0418 φ + 0.00056 T − 0.0029
φ 2

The goodness of fit of MLR and RSM is assessed using four well-known statistical indicators listed in Table 2 . According to Table 2, the RSM model regarding the highest correlation coefficient (R = 0.984) and most minor error metrics (RMSE = 0.013 W/m.K and MAPE = 4.593%) has better performance than the MLR model (R = 0.960, RMSE = 0.021 W/m.K, and MAPE = 7.647%) for the testing stage in predicting the thermal conductivity of hybrid NF.

Fig. 8 exhibits the scatter plot (Left side) and trend variation plot (Right side) of experimental and predicted thermal conductivity values. The scatter plots comparison ascertained that the predicted value via the RSM, on the one hand, has a better agreement with the experimental values and, on the other hand, has the slightest deviation from the identity line (1:1). Besides, the right side of Fig. 8 showed that the RSM perfectly captures the non-linearity of data points. However, the results of MLR are promising and acceptable given accuracy and compatibility.

Fig. 9 depicted the violin plot (probability distribution function) of relative error (RD %) for both presented data-driven models to assess the reliability of the results. The quartile criteria ascertained that both models regarding the low range of RD (RSM|13.63% and MLR|22.94%) for the testing phase yield acceptable accuracy. However, the RSM based on lower (Q25% = −5.386% and Q75% = 2.539%) has better predictive performance than MLR in terms of (Q25% = −12.96% and Q75% = 3.959%). As the last visualized validation stage, the 3D contours of RD of each model versus two independent parameters, namely volume fraction
( φ )
and temperature (T) were illustrated in Fig. 10 . It can be included that for the modeling of the thermal conductivity of hybrid NF via the RSM model, the most RD values are fallen in the range of (
1 % ≤ φ ≤ 2 %
and
4 % ≤ φ ≤ 5 %
) whereas, for the MLR model, the extreme values of RD are fallen in the range of
(
1 % ≤ φ ≤ 3 %
)
.

In the present study, the PEG-CD NF was synthesized and several experiments were performed to measure its thermal conductivity at different temperatures and
φ
s. The considered
φ
values were 0, 1%, 3% and 7%, while the considered temperatures were 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C. The results showed an increase in the thermal conductivity of the NF with increasing temperature and
φ
. It was found that the increase in temperature and
φ
increase the thermal conductivity up to 13.43% and 85.17%, respectively. Moreover, the modeling results via RSM and MLR approaches ascertained that RSM owing to optimal metric performances (R = 0.984 and MAPE = 4.593%), provided more accuracy than MLR (R = 0.960 and MAPE = 7.647%). The diagnostic analysis in the testing stage proved that the RSM model in the account of less RD-range (13.63%) yielded more reliable outcomes than the MLR model (22.94%). Besides, each approach correlated a precise relationship for estimating the thermal conductivity of PEG-CD NF.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancing thermal conductivity of fluids with nanoparticles
